evidence_quotes.quote: Video games are the prime example of this.
The operator reserves the right to terminate this service at any time and to render the product completely unusable.

The current practice of taking out sold software is absolutely anti-consumer (and anti-scientific) and would not simply be tolerated for any other product (where else someone who sells a product to me can simply decide to destroy it after any time?).
end-of-life plan with their products mandatory

paragraphs.text: Digital fairness is ever more important in today’s time. The rights of technical groups vis-à-vis consumers are overwhelming in many areas. An important area that should be taken into account is the practice that “purchased” software can arbitrarily be rendered unusable by publishers. Video games are the prime example of this. Nowadays, it is going and walking to ‘sell’ games that rely on the active service of the publisher. The operator reserves the right to terminate this service at any time and to render the product completely unusable. These are not rent or time licences, but, according to all legal circumstances, a purchase of goods – including software/games sold as physical goods, e.g. on disks. And even though there are sufficient technical solutions to avoid this. Software and specific games that rely on online servers, for example, can be released from compulsion with patches, or software could be made available to users to operate these servers on their own. The failure to do so has led to hundreds of programmes/games simply being rendered unusable. Most often, as the publisher did not want to make the effort, or often even active, to force users to buy the successor product. The possibility that the ‘purchased’ software can be destroyed at any time is not communicated at the time of purchase and the deadlines are absolutely arbitrary. In the case of games in particular, there are known cases where a game is only stopped after years or within two weeks of its appearance. The consumer has no opportunity to denounce this or to defend himself against it – although the legal situation here is far from clear. In addition to the user-law aspect, it can also be noted that video games in particular involve a large amount of art work that deserves protection. Music, animations, textures, the overall experience: it is also morally highly questionable to destroy this unilaterally and to let the creators’ efforts disappear forever. It is therefore important to add to the Digital Fairness Act the requirement for publishers of software and special video games that rely on active support to make an end-of-life plan with their products mandatory, so that they can continue to be used in an acceptable manner after buyers have discontinued the support. The exact form of this can remain open. The current practice of taking out sold software is absolutely anti-consumer (and anti-scientific) and would not simply be tolerated for any other product (where else someone who sells a product to me can simply decide to destroy it after any time?). So if digital fairness is to be pushed forward, this is an important and indispensable complement that must be considered.
